text: This article is part of the international law in context debate. By examining the practical workings of international economic law in the context of the increasingly complex and multi-layered disputes arising in connection with investment activity, this article, through an interdisciplinary doctrinal and socio-legal lens and based on the *Eco Oro v. Colombia* ISAB, seeks to shed light on the workings of investment disputes that engage multiple layers of legal relationships with potentially incommensurable content and to look at the formal jurisdiction of the arbitration tribunal alongside the everyday social activities that it encompasses, to the effect of bringing to the fore the highly political nature of an arbitration institution that serves to reshape actors, space, the environment and livelihoods through the prism of an international legal framework tailored to safeguard foreign investments, instead of being working towards a reform of the system so as to move from the current primacy given to the protection of investments to a more comprehensive investment governance regime where the social, political and environmental elements inherent to foreign investment activities are fully taken into account.
